critique.summary: The article provides a detailed overview of the new features, previews, and deprecations introduced in Java 26, highlighting improvements in JVM performance, concurrency, HTTP client support, cryptographic APIs, and language enhancements. It emphasizes Java 26 as a foundational release preparing for future major updates, especially related to Project Valhalla.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new HotSpot JVM features like ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector (JEP 516) and improved G1 garbage collector throughput by reducing synchronization (JEP 522).
The HTTP Client API now supports HTTP/3 (JEP 517), allowing faster and more reliable HTTP communications with fallback mechanisms to older protocols.

Several preview features were reintroduced with minor changes, including PEM encoding for cryptographic objects (JEP 524), structured concurrency for better task management (JEP 525), lazy constants for deferred immutable initialization (JEP 526), and primitive types in pattern matching and switch statements (JEP 530).
The Vector API continues incubation with no changes from Java 25, enabling efficient SIMD vector computations (JEP 529).
Java 26 deprecates the ability to mutate final fields via deep reflection, aiming to improve program correctness and JVM optimizations (JEP 500).
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reflects the official Java Enhancement Proposals (JEPs) and features introduced in Java 26 as documented by the OpenJDK project and other authoritative Java sources. The descriptions of new APIs, JVM improvements, and language features align with publicly available JEP documents and Java release notes. The article provides technical explanations consistent with the official specifications and does not make unsupported claims.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential migration challenges or backward compatibility issues developers might face when adopting Java 26 features.
It does not mention the impact of these changes on existing Java frameworks or libraries, which may require updates to leverage new features or handle deprecations.
The article assumes familiarity with Java internals and JEP terminology, which might limit accessibility for less experienced developers.
There is no discussion on the adoption rate or ecosystem readiness for features like HTTP/3 or structured concurrency.
Security implications of changes, especially around cryptographic PEM encoding and final field mutation restrictions, are not explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around recent Java 26 updates and the broader Java ecosystem, including its culture, technical improvements like virtual threads and sealed interfaces, and the relationship with Android development. Some comments praise Java's technical maturity, while others critique its community culture and Android's lagging support.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights recent Java language features and improvements, showing a good understanding of the language's evolution and the significance of virtual threads.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: The removal of the Applet API is a factual update, and the comment provides relevant historical context about Java plugins and their challenges, especially on Linux.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: The comment mixes subjective cultural observations with a reasonable claim about Java's industry standing; however, the cultural critique is opinion-based and less verifiable.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about Android's Java support and Kotlin's influence, though it lacks definitive answers and mixes speculation with fact.

comments.author: wiseowise
comments.text: Android as always in shambles. Shame that neither Google, nor ecosystem cares about Java because they’ve bought JetBrains kopium.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration about Android's Java ecosystem and Google's focus on Kotlin (JetBrains), reflecting a common sentiment but stated in a confrontational tone.

comments.author: haolez
comments.text: I was pretty surprised when I learned recently that the Java alternative for green threads doesn't use colored functions. It put Java in a higher place in my perception.
comments.analysis: Provides a personal insight about Java's concurrency model, which is accurate but anecdotal and less substantive than other comments.

comments.author: badgersnake
comments.text: Vector API (Eleventh Incubator) - maybe 11th time’s the charm.
comments.analysis: Briefly notes the long incubation of the Vector API, a factual but minimal contribution to the discussion.
